evidence.reviewer_note: V trap avoided: 60%+ of wage bill is physical field labor (37-3011, 37-1012, 37-3013, 37-3012, 47-2061) assigned 0% automatable — this is the defining structural feature. The 15.65% Damodaran margin used for n_band is generous for commercial-maintenance-heavy operators (BrightView runs 11.7% adjusted EBITDA); the true n_band is likely lower, which would reduce B. The buy_mult of 5.5x applies to band-size firms ($3-4M implied EBITDA) on a normalized EBITDA basis; smaller mow-and-blow operators transact at 2-3x SDE, which is irrelevant to the band but shows wide multiple variation within the code. The ai_replaceable_share of ~8% of total labor is deliberately conservative per the prompt's explicit instruction that robotic mowing is a capex story not folded into the AI-labor channel. S = 1.96 reflects a genuine low-score code: high B (active consolidation, succession pressure), but very low V (physical labor dominates), very low A (fragmented, slow-adopting industry), and moderate-low C/M.

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Physical chairside assistance and sterilization are not AI-replaceable. The only automatable sliver is clinical notes dictation and charting documentation (5%). State practice acts define this role's physical duties.

Licensed, physical chairside care (prophylaxis, perio maintenance) under state practice act supervision requirements — not AI-substitutable. Only a small documentation/notes fraction is automatable (3%).

Principal AI-exposed cost base: scheduling, recall reminders, insurance verification, prior authorizations, patient communication, eligibility checks. AI scheduling tools and automated patient outreach substantially reduce labor. In-person reception handoffs and complex insurance appeals persist. 65% reflects the large automatable task share.

Licensed clinical procedures (restorations, extractions, surgery) are physically reserved and legally restricted to licensed dentists. AI diagnostic tools (Overjet, Pearl — FDA-cleared radiograph analysis) augment detection but do not replace clinical time. The automatable sliver is treatment planning documentation, note generation, and coding assistance (4%). Note: owner-dentists may take income as distributions rather than W-2 wages, understating this wage share, but the automatable fraction remains very low regardless of compensation form.

Appointment scheduling, phone intake, appointment reminders, patient communication — majority automatable with AI scheduling/communication tools. Physical patient check-in and face-to-face reception are residual (40% non-automatable).

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Revenue cycle oversight, reporting, and administrative performance monitoring partially automate. HR management, payer contract negotiations, and staff leadership persist. 30% reflects the operational reporting/analytics automation slice.
Data entry, filing, general administrative tasks — predominantly AI-automatable. Small residual in physical document handling and in-office tasks.

Insurance claims submission, payment posting, EOB reconciliation, and routine denial management are highly automatable with AI-driven revenue cycle management tools. Complex appeal management and payer negotiations persist. 75% reflects the largely automated billing workflow.

Revenue cycle reporting, performance dashboards, and scheduling analytics partially automate. HR management, payer contracting, compliance, and strategic leadership persist. 20% reflects the reporting/analytics sliver.
Similar to medical secretaries — scheduling, correspondence, document preparation predominantly automatable. 65%.

breakdown_by_role.rationale: In-house lab techs and sterilization techs perform physical work. CAD/CAM milling is a capex channel distinct from AI-labor substitution. Marketing has some AI exposure. 10% reflects a small marketing/communications sliver.

pi_dist.rationale: Dental revenue arrives through three channels: PPO fixed fee schedules (~57% of revenue per DE/Levin 2024 annual practice survey — 'typical payer mix was 57% insurance patients and 43% fee-for-service'), Medicaid (~5-10% at participating practices, reimbursement well below private rates per ADA HPI 2024), and fee-for-service/self-pay (~33-38%). Under PPO fee schedules, fees are set contractually by the insurer, so AI-driven efficiency savings accrue to the practice as margin improvement (not passed to insurers who cannot ratchet fees downward on individual practices short-term). FFS revenue is fully practice-controlled. Patient relationships are practice-owned and highly durable — dental families maintain multi-year attachment to practice and treating dentist. Corporate-practice-of-dentistry statutes in most states require dentist ownership, reinforcing direct practice-patient relationship. Moderate: 0.50, reflecting the mix of payer-constrained (PPO) and practice-controlled (FFS) revenue, and strong patient relationship ownership.
sources: 2024 DE/Levin Group Annual Practice Survey (Dental Economics)
ADA Health Policy Institute Dental Payer Mix data (ada.org)
ADA HPI Medicaid Reimbursement for Dental Care 2024 (ada.org)

pi_moat.rationale: Strong moat factors: (1) licensure — dental procedures legally reserved to licensed dentists, no AI bypass possible for physical care; (2) switching costs — dental patient relationships are multi-year/multi-family, radiograph history creates data lock-in; (3) physical facility and equipment — significant sunk costs and operational infrastructure; (4) practice act restrictions on corporate ownership limit competitive form. Moderated by: (1) PPO network insurer can steer patients to other in-network providers; (2) DSO competition may introduce lower-cost alternatives; (3) SmileDirectClub's collapse (2023) confirms teledentistry/mail-order bypass limited in scope — patients cannot substitute away from operatory visits. No generic AI bypass threat since dental care is physically delivered. Rated 0.55.
sources: ADA Health Policy Institute Dentist Workforce 2024 (ada.org)
CT Acquisitions Dental DSO M&A Multiples 2026 (ctacquisitions.com)

t50_years.derivation: Current material AI adoption in dental practice workflows (scheduling, billing, front-desk automation): estimated 11-15% based on ADA 2025 survey ('fewer than 11% have embedded AI in clinical or operational workflows in a measurable way'); broader 35% includes light use. Material/embedded use = 12% estimate. Historical analog: dental EHR/practice management software became widely available ~2000-2005 and reached ~50% adoption by ~2012, i.e., ~10 years to 50%. However, dental adoption is slower than medical due to absence of federal mandates (HITECH Act did not mandate dental EHR adoption). AI tools face cost, learning-curve, and change-management barriers identical to EHR — but with lower capital requirements (SaaS vs installed software). Starting from 2026 with ~12% material adoption, reaching 50% by ~2035 implies t50 = 9 years. This is slightly longer than EHR adoption due to the fragmented small-practice base and higher skepticism (validated by ADA survey showing 47% tested but only 11% embedded).

current_adoption_pct.source: ADA Health Policy Institute 2025 practice trends survey; 30 Dental Tech Adoption Stats (Clerri); Overjet 2025 dental trends
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: ADA 2025 survey: 'more than 47% of US dental practices have tested at least one AI tool in the past 18 months, but fewer than 11% have embedded AI in their clinical or operational workflows in a measurable way.' Clerri: '35% of practices have implemented some form of AI-powered technology.' Material/embedded adoption estimated at 12% (midpoint between ADA 11% embedded and Clerri 35% broader measure, weighting ADA primary-source embedded-use figure more heavily).

historical_analogs.value: Dental EHR/practice management software reached ~50% adoption approximately 10 years after broad availability (~2000 to 2012)
historical_analogs.source: PMC research on electronic dental record adoption; Maryland MHCC dental EHR adoption report 2018
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: EHR adoption study: 'In 2012, only 52% of U.S. dental practices used any form of electronic record system for patient care' — marking ~50% penetration approximately 10 years after broad availability (~2000-2005). 'Dental EHR adoption trails medical EHR adoption by nearly 20 percent.' No federal mandate for dental EHR, paralleling the AI adoption environment.

owners_60plus_pct.source: ADA Health Policy Institute Dentist Workforce statistics 2024 (ada.org)
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: ADA HPI 2024: 18.7% of all dentists ages 55-64; 15.5% ages 65+. Total dentists 60+ estimated at ~24.85% of all dentists (half of 55-64 band + 65+). Practice-owning dentists skew older: 48% of dentists with 25+ years in practice are in solo practice vs 15% of dentists under 10 years out — owners are predominantly mid-to-late career. owners_60plus_pct estimated at 35% given the ownership skew toward older dentists (practice owners disproportionately 50+ given declining ownership among younger cohorts).

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: ADA HPI (2024): 'The share of U.S. dentists affiliated with a DSO has more than doubled since 2015, reaching 16.1% in 2024 — more pronounced among younger dentists (27% of dentists <10 years out).' Practice ownership among all dentists declining from 84.7% (2005) to 72.5% (2024). Fewer younger dentists buying practices from retiring owners, driving DSO acquisition demand and documented succession shortage in the independent-practice segment. Average dentist retirement age is 68.7 (2024), meaning older owners who want to sell face a shrinking pool of independent-practice buyers.

active_consolidators.source: CT Acquisitions Dental DSO PE Roll-Up Tracker 2026 (ctacquisitions.com)
active_consolidators.figure_quoted: CT Acquisitions tracker profiles 21 PE-backed dental DSO platforms with verified acquisition activity in 2024-2026: Heartland Dental (KKR), Aspen Dental (Leonard Green + Ares), Pacific Dental Services, MB2 Dental (Charlesbank + Warburg Pincus), Smile Brands (New Mountain), Mortenson (Audax + Genstar), Dental Care Alliance (Quad-C), Sage Dental (Carousel), Western Dental (New Mountain), Affordable Care (Berkshire), Specialty Dental Brands (Linden), U.S. Oral Surgery Management (OMERS), Smile Doctors (Linden), Endodontic Practice Partners (Sun Capital), Children's Dental Health (DFW Capital), Great Expressions (Roark), Dental365 (Jordan), Allied OMS (65 Equity), Beacon Oral Specialists (Blue Sea), ProSmile (acquired 2024), ImmediaDent/Imagn (Audax). Additional 40-60+ platforms with less disclosure.

buy_mult.source: CT Acquisitions Dental DSO M&A Multiples Report 2026; FOCUS Bankers Dental Practice EBITDA Multiples 2026; Provident Healthcare Partners
buy_mult.figure_quoted: CT Acquisitions: 'solo owner-operator general practices under $500K SDE traded at 4.0x to 6.0x SDE.' FOCUS Bankers: 'under $1M EBITDA: 5-7x; $1-3M EBITDA: 7-9x adjusted EBITDA.' EBITDA normalization applied: small single-location practices report SDE inflated by owner-dentist clinical compensation; after deducting market-rate associate cost (~30% of doctor's collections), normalized EBITDA is substantially lower. For band-size add-on acquisitions in the $1-3M normalized EBITDA range, the applicable range is 5-8x adjusted EBITDA. Median: 6.5x normalized EBITDA for single-location or small multi-site add-on acquisitions in the band.

exit_mult.source: CT Acquisitions Dental DSO M&A Multiples Report 2026; Skytale Group; Provident Healthcare Partners
exit_mult.figure_quoted: CT Acquisitions: 'platform-tier DSO targets with $10.0M or greater adjusted EBITDA traded in a 10.0x to 15.0x adjusted EBITDA band during 2024 through Q2 2026.' FOCUS Bankers: '$5M+ EBITDA: 11x+ (select cases); platform exits: 9-11x EBITDA.' Using 11.0x as conservative median for $10M+ EBITDA platform exits, reflecting the lower end of the 10-15x range and supported by the FOCUS Bankers 9-11x platform exit figure.

pricing_structure.value: PPO fixed fee schedules (~57% of revenue — insurer sets fees, practice is in-network at negotiated rates); Medicaid/government (~5-10% at participating practices — fixed state schedules well below commercial rates); FFS/self-pay (~33-38% — practice sets own fees, full UCR collected). AI efficiency savings primarily accrue to practice as margin under fixed-fee-schedule and FFS arrangements; PPO insurers do not ratchet down individual practice rates based on efficiency. However, large PPO networks and leasing network aggregators can compress fee schedules system-wide on renewal. Payer mix is heavily PPO/insurance-dependent; teledentistry and mail-order alternatives have shown limited demand substitution (SmileDirectClub bankruptcy 2023 as evidence).
pricing_structure.source: 2024 DE/Levin Group Annual Practice Survey (Dental Economics); ADA Health Policy Institute payer mix data
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: DE/Levin 2024 survey: 'typical practice payer mix was 57% insurance patients and 43% fee-for-service.' PPO plans dominate insurance segment. FFS produces 30-45% more revenue per patient than in-network PPO due to full UCR billing.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: uplift_pp = V_raw × 100 = 6.51pp. A = 1.111 (slow adoption, t50 = 9 years). Uplift > 3pp and A < 5, so no tension flag applies. The modest V (2.60) reflects a meaningful automatable surface (~14% of labor) but the vast majority of labor (dentists, hygienists, assistants — ~72% of wage bill) is physically and legally protected from AI substitution. The low A is consistent with historically slow dental technology adoption and the absence of federal mandates.

terminal_value.justification: Dental care is delivered chairside by licensed clinicians — ~72% of the wage bill (dentists, hygienists, dental assistants) performs physical procedures that mature AI does not replace, and state practice acts legally reserve them. Visit demand is not substitutable via teledentistry for the full scope of dental care; SmileDirectClub's 2023 bankruptcy is direct evidence of the limits of remote/mail-order substitution for clinical dental care. AI diagnostics (Overjet, Pearl radiograph analysis) are FDA-cleared for augmentation, not replacement — they improve detection rates, which may increase treated volume rather than reduce visits. Front-office automation removes labor cost without reducing patient demand or visit volume. DURABLE: demand survives mature AI; AI cuts back-office and administrative cost only, does not substitute the core clinical product.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V (2.60) was derived from OEWS wage-share breakdown with within-role automatable fractions assessed on task content — no reference to DSO deal activity or PE consolidation trends. The 4% automatable fraction for dentists specifically notes AI diagnostic tools augment but do not reduce dentist time, grounded in FDA-cleared indications for Overjet/Pearl (augmentation of radiograph reading, not replacement). A (1.111) was derived from current adoption rate (ADA HPI 2025: <11% embedded workflow adoption) and historical EHR analog (50% dental EHR adoption ~10 years from broad availability). No reference to DSO consolidation activity or M&A trends in either derivation. Confirmed: V and A are justified independently.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Payer mix: ~57% PPO insurance (fixed contracted fee schedules set by insurer), ~5-10% Medicaid (fixed state schedules), ~33-38% FFS/self-pay (practice-set fees at full UCR). Under PPO fixed-fee arrangements, AI efficiency savings do not flow to payers short-term — the insurer cannot ratchet down individual practice fees based on that practice's cost improvements (fees are set network-wide on multi-year contracts). However, PPO lease network aggregators and large insurers can compress system-wide fees at renewal. FFS revenue gives the practice full margin capture. Reflected in pi_dist = 0.50: moderate practice control over patient relationship and revenue, but PPO fee-schedule constraints and the potential for payer steering partially limit full price-setting power. pi_moat = 0.55 reflects the licensure, switching-cost, and practice-act moats that prevent AI-enabled competitors from bypassing the practice-patient relationship.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: A = 10/t50; at t50=6 → A=1.667; at t50=14 → A=0.714. S varies from ~2.44 (t50=14) to ~2.86 (t50=6). Moderate impact on S.

M = clamp(4×(11-buy)/buy, 0, 10); at buy=5.0 → M=4.8; at buy=8.0 → M=1.5. S varies from ~2.17 (buy=8.0) to ~3.22 (buy=5.0). Highest single input impact — EBITDA normalization assumption for band-size dental groups is the key uncertainty.
M at exit=9.0, buy=6.5 → M=1.538; at exit=14.0 → M=4.615. S varies from ~2.36 (exit=9.0) to ~3.14 (exit=14.0). Significant impact.

evidence.reviewer_note: EBITDA normalization is the defining trap for this code: owner-dentist clinical production sits inside 'profit' on an SDE basis, and normalizing for market-rate associate compensation (28-32% of doctor's collections) materially reduces the margin. The Damodaran 15.8% margin for n_band is a rough proxy; normalized dental group EBITDA may be 10-18% depending on ownership structure. The buy_mult of 6.5x normalized EBITDA is a judgment call across a wide range (the 5-8x add-on range from FOCUS Bankers); using SDE multiples without normalization would overstate buy_mult. The role_mix is at 4-digit 6212, which is essentially co-extensive with 621210 (no contamination from general medicine). Specialist dentists (29-1022/1023/1024/1029 orthodontists, oral surgeons, etc.) sit in the unlisted tail with their higher wage rates — the listed 29-1021 Dentists General wage share of 25.26% likely understates total dentist compensation share. If specialist wages add ~5% to the dentist wage share (at 0.04 automatable fraction), ai_replaceable_share increases by only ~0.002 — immaterial. The n_band of 369 is flagged in the prompt as a likely floor since 20-99 employee class practices clearing $1M EBITDA at true group margins are missed; true n_band could be 500-1,500, which would increase TD and B meaningfully. S = 2.66 is a modest but above-kill score, driven primarily by strong B (active DSO consolidation, documented succession shortage) and moderate C (payer-mix diversity, licensure moat), offset by slow AI adoption (low A).

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Licensed hands-on care; billing unit IS therapist time in 15-min CPT units — replacing clinical labor destroys the billed product. Only documentation time is AI-automatable: AI scribes reclaim ~7% of this role's labor hours from note-writing, which converts to throughput (more visits per day) not headcount reduction. Within-role fraction capped at documentation share of therapist workday (~30-40 min/day out of ~8hr shift = ~6-8%).
Same hands-on structure as PTs but with smaller documentation burden relative to their scope; Medicare 85% modifier already constrains their economics independently of AI.

Licensed clinical time billed per session; teletherapy shifted some delivery online but remains human-delivered. Documentation AI applicable at same ~7% fraction as PT. Teletherapy channel (school-based SLP) may have slightly higher AI-scribing uptake but clinical delivery remains human.
Hands-on functional rehabilitation; same billing structure as PT. Documentation automation is the only AI-surface inside this role.

Room setup, equipment turnover, scheduling support — these non-clinical functions are partially absorbable by AI-assisted scheduling, self-serve check-in, and automated room/equipment tracking. Cannot eliminate the role (physical presence required) but can reduce per-clinic headcount.

Core AI surface: scheduling, insurance eligibility verification, prior authorization paperwork, patient communication. Prior-auth automation is directly relevant given MA plans' heavy therapy utilization management. 30% residual for human judgment in complex authorization escalations and referring-physician relationship tasks.
Hands-on clinical extenders performing physical therapeutic activities under OT supervision. Very small AI-automatable slice (documentation notes only).
Compliance reporting, revenue-cycle analytics, scheduling oversight — these automate materially (~20%). Referral-relationship management, strategic decisions, staff oversight persist as human work.

Scheduling calls, appointment reminders, patient intake, insurance confirmation — heavily AI-automatable via conversational AI and scheduling bots. 30% residual for in-person greeting and complex patient navigation.
Billing, claims submission, denial management, coding review — strong AI surface. Denial management AI is already commercially deployed in rehab billing.

Long tail includes athletic trainers, exercise physiologists, marketing staff, HR/recruiting — mixed clinical-adjacent and admin roles. ~15% automatable on average (marketing analytics, recruiting screening, HR paperwork). Physical/clinical roles dominate this tail and are not automatable.

pi_dist.rationale: Therapy revenue is billed on a per-procedure/timed-CPT basis set by CMS fee schedule and commercial payer contracts — operators cannot convert AI efficiency gains directly into price increases. Patient flow is gated by physician referral networks, health-system narrow networks, and MA plan preferred-provider lists rather than direct patient choice. Direct-access patients (who choose their clinic) represent a minority of volume. Clinic-level brand and relationship continuity provide partial protection, but payer-side mechanisms and referral gatekeeping significantly constrain how much of the AI value flows to the operator rather than the payer (via rate-rebasing) or the referral gatekeeper.
sources: CMS Physician Fee Schedule final rule 2025 (conversion factor -2.83% to $32.35 for 2025, fifth consecutive annual cut)
APTA article 2025-11-18: CMS finalizes fee schedule pay bump for first time in 5 years
CMS PDGM behavioral adjustment mechanism (documented payer clawback of measured efficiency gains)

pi_moat.rationale: Physical licensure (PT, OT, SLP, AuD) creates real barriers: only licensed professionals may deliver and bill for services. Physical presence in clinic space is required; generic AI cannot substitute. Patient-therapist relationships create meaningful switching costs. However: entry into outpatient rehab is accessible (lease clinical space, hire licensed staff, obtain Medicare provider number — no CON required in most states); the profession produces ~14,000 new PT graduates/year keeping supply flowing; Hinge Health and Sword Health (digital MSK platforms) represent a genuine substitution threat for low-acuity chronic pain visits sold through employers/payers. Moat is real but moderate.
sources: State PT licensure requirements (all 50 states require PT license for service delivery and billing)
Hinge Health IPO prospectus and utilization data: 6.8x more outpatient visits in control vs. Hinge group (2024 Medicare cost study)
CT Acquisitions M&A guide 2026: entry barriers analysis

t50_years.derivation: current_adoption_pct = 0.33 (Prompt Health 2025 survey: ~1/3 of 550 practices use automation tools materially; ~2/3 use AI for documentation specifically as of late 2025). Historical analog: EHR/EMR adoption in small specialty practices went from ~5% (2009) to ~50% (2017) in ~8 years. AI documentation is moving faster — from ~5% (2022) to ~33% (late 2025) in ~3 years, implying ~9.3 percentage points per year. From 33% to 50% = 17pp ÷ 9.3pp/yr = ~1.8 years from late 2025, or roughly mid-2027. However, the Raintree survey conflates pilots/intentions with material deployment; Prompt Health's more conservative 1/3 estimate likely better captures clinics where AI is workflow-integrated (not just occasionally used). Adjusting for laggard tail and workflow integration vs. pilot: t50 = 4 years from today (mid-2026 to mid-2030). Small independent clinics (the majority of n_band) adopt more slowly than larger groups. Range 2-7 years.

No retainer or value-based pricing dominant.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: V_raw = 8.036pp of revenue. EBITDA margin is 15.80%, so AI uplift represents 50.9% of current EBITDA — substantial but concentrated in the non-clinical admin/documentation fringe (~10-15% of revenue) and therapist throughput gain. A = 2.50 (t50=4 years, moderate pace). With slow adoption, the uplift is real but arrives gradually; no inconsistency — a low-A score with moderate-V is coherent when structural barriers (owner inertia, small-clinic cash constraints, workflow disruption) slow deployment.

terminal_value.justification: Demand for licensed in-person therapy is durable (aging demographics, physical-presence requirement). But CMS conversion-factor cuts (5 consecutive annual reductions through 2025) and MA prior-auth systematically transfer efficiency gains to payers. Hinge Health's 2024 Medicare utilization study shows 6.8x more outpatient visits in control vs. Hinge users — measurable visit diversion at low-acuity MSK margin. AI throughput gains (documentation time reclaimed → more visits) likely get partially absorbed in next rate rebasing cycle. Terminal value PRESSURED: demand survives but operator margins are structurally squeezed by payer rate-setting and digital substitution at the periphery.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V (3.214) derived from labor_share (BLS/Census dataset input) × ai_replaceable_share (0.1338, computed from OEWS role-mix fractions and declared within-role automatable fractions based on task analysis of documentation time, scheduling, and billing functions). No reference to PT M&A deal activity, PE consolidation volume, or add-on counts in V or A. A (2.50) derived from t50=4 years, which was estimated from current_adoption_pct (Prompt Health survey) and historical EMR analog — no consolidation evidence used.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Billing is predominantly per-procedure/per-visit via government and commercial fee schedules (not cost-plus or hourly rate-pass-through). In principle, operators keep AI-driven margin improvement within rate cycles — AI documentation savings flow to the clinic, not to payers, until the next fee-schedule update. However, CMS demonstrably resets rates to recapture measured efficiency (as documented in PT fee-schedule trajectory and analogous to PDGM behavioral adjustments in home health). This prospective-but-capped structure is reflected in pi_dist=0.35: the operator captures value in the near term but structural rate-rebasing limits long-run retention. MA plans' utilization management (prior auth, visit caps) is a second deflation mechanism. pi_moat=0.45 reflects that licensure and physical-presence requirements protect against direct bypass but do not prevent payer-side value capture.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: At t50=2: A=5.0, S≈4.2; at t50=7: A=1.43, S≈2.6. t50 is the highest-leverage single input — a 5-year range in t50 spans nearly 2 full composite-score points.
At 0.09: V_raw=0.054, V=2.16, S≈2.8; at 0.18: V_raw=0.108, V=4.32, S≈3.3. The billing-unit trap constrains the upper bound — clinical roles cannot be replaced without destroying revenue.
At 0.22: OW_base=0.343+0.1=0.443, B≈5.5; at 0.40: OW_base=0.857+0.1=0.9(capped), B≈6.8. B changes by ~1.3 points across this range, S changes ~0.3 points.
At buy=5.0, exit=12: M=clamp(4×7/5,0,10)=5.6; at buy=8.0, exit=12: M=clamp(4×4/8,0,10)=2.0. S shifts from ~2.8 to ~3.5 depending on buy multiple.

evidence.reviewer_note: Heterogeneity: NAICS 621340 spans outpatient ortho PT (dominant sub-segment by clinic count and revenue), pediatric speech/language (school contracts, Medicaid), OT hand therapy, and audiology (hearing-aid retail margin). n_band 821 = 460 firms (class 100-499) + 361 firms (class 500+). Dominant sub-segment scored: outpatient musculoskeletal/neurological PT (largest revenue share). Audiology notably differs — hearing-aid sales carry retail margins and a product-revenue model distinct from the time-based therapy model; if the 361 class-500+ firms skew toward audiology chains, V would be understated for PT and overstated for audiology. Billing-unit trap applied: within-role automatable fraction for licensed clinical roles (PT, OT, SLP, PTA, OTA) capped at documentation-time fraction (~4-7%), not at administrative-work share, because reducing licensed-therapist clinical hours reduces billable CPT units. Digital MSK substitution (Hinge Health IPO May 2025; 6.8x outpatient-visit differential in 2024 Medicare cost study) is a growing threat to low-acuity chronic LBP volume that is not captured in current revenue figures but represents a headwind to long-run V realization. USPH Metro acquisition at 12.75x EBITDA for a 50-clinic MSO (Oct 2024) serves as primary exit multiple anchor.

breakdown_by_role.role: Home Health and Personal Care Aides (31-1120)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: The product itself — bathing, transfers, meal prep, companionship in the client's home. Mature AI cannot replace physical in-home care. Sensor/remote-monitoring technology may trim visit frequency at the margin but the binding constraint is aide supply (79% annual turnover), not productivity. Setting fraction to 0.00 per billing-unit trap analog: replacing aide time destroys the billed service.

Skilled home visits are physical and cannot be AI-replaced. However, a substantial share of RN time is OASIS assessments, care-plan documentation, and coding-adjacent paperwork — AI documentation tools (NLP-assisted OASIS completion, automated care-plan drafting) are commercially deployed in home health EMRs. Estimate ~15% of RN labor hours is documentation-intensive work current AI can substantially reduce. The visit itself persists.

breakdown_by_role.role: Licensed Practical and Licensed Vocational Nurses (29-2061)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Hands-on clinical support in the home; smaller documentation component than RNs. Narrow AI surface for charting support only.
Hands-on personal care tasks in the home; minimal AI-automatable component. Fraction reflects only de minimis charting/check-in flows.

Home-visit PT: licensed hands-on rehabilitation. Only documentation automatable (~7% of role labor hours). PDGM removed therapy-volume incentives in 2020 — visit counts already fell for payment-policy reasons; do not attribute that trend to AI.

QA review, scheduling oversight, utilization reporting, compliance monitoring — these functions automate substantially with AI analytics tools. Family communication and clinical escalation decisions persist as human work. Scheduling in home health is operationally complex (distributed workforce matching to patient geography/needs) — AI scheduling is a real but partial automation.
Utilization management documentation, discharge planning coordination, benefits navigation — partially automatable. Patient counseling and clinical escalation judgment persist as human work.
Billing, claims submission, EVV compliance reporting, denial management — core AI surface. AI coding tools and EVV automation are directly deployable.

Intake/referral processing, eligibility checks, scheduling coordination, payer authorizations — high AI surface. The EVV compliance layer and prior-auth for MA plans add documented administrative burden addressable by AI.
Home-visit OT: licensed hands-on care; only documentation automatable at the same ~7% fraction as home-visit PT.

HR/recruiting staff, marketing liaisons, chaplains/hospice-adjacent roles, IT support. AI-assisted recruiting/retention tooling is a real lever given 79% annual aide turnover — screening, onboarding automation, shift-fill matching. Hospital-liaison marketing persists as relationship work. Blend estimated at 12% automatable.

pi_dist.rationale: Revenue flows via government and negotiated rates, not operator-set prices. Under Medicare PDGM (prospective 30-day episodes), agencies keep efficiency gains within a cycle but CMS behavioral-adjustment cuts (-3.925% in 2023, -2.890% in 2024, -1.975% in 2025, -1.023% in 2026) are an explicitly documented recurring mechanism transferring measured savings to payers. Medicaid HCBS rates are state-set; several states have wage-pass-through laws (NY wage parity) that convert labor-cost efficiency directly into mandated wage floors, further constraining margin retention. MA negotiated per-visit rates reprice annually with no guaranteed efficiency pass-through. Referral flow owned by hospital discharge planners, ACO networks, and MA preferred-network lists — agencies cannot build durable direct consumer relationships independent of these gatekeepers. pi_dist = 0.30: meaningful structural value destruction at the payer and gatekeeper layers.
sources: CMS CY2025 Home Health PPS Final Rule (CMS-1803-F): permanent behavioral adjustment -1.975% for 2025
CMS CY2026 Federal Register (2025-21767): permanent adjustment -1.023% for 2026
Applied Policy: CMS Finalizes Payment Cuts for Home Health Agencies for CY 2025
MedPAC July 2024 Data Book: home health payments persistently exceed costs; MedPAC recommends cuts

pi_moat.rationale: Medicare-certified HHA requires CMS certification and state licensure; several high-population states (NY, IL, FL) have licensure moratoria or CON requirements restricting new entrants in the Medicare-certified skilled care segment — real barrier for the dominant n_band sub-segment. Physical presence in the home is required; generic AI cannot bypass the agency. However: non-medical personal care (substantial portion of 621610) has low entry barriers in most states. Aide workforce supply is the operational constraint — 79% annual turnover means the asset base is fragile and hard to protect. Referral-network lock-in creates switching costs for discharge planners but reprice via MA contracting. No proprietary data moat. Overall: moderate barrier from Medicare certification and state moratoria, offset by personal-care low barriers and workforce fragility.
sources: CMS Home Health Agency certification requirements
State CON/licensure moratorium states: NY, IL, FL (documented in industry M&A guides)
Activated Insights 2025 Benchmarking Report: caregiver turnover 75-79% annually
myEZCare 2026 Home Care Industry Report: 79% annual aide turnover

t50_years.derivation: current_adoption_pct = 0.11 (11% of home care agencies running AI in full production per HHCN 2025 research; 24% have made any AI investment). Historical analog: EVV (Electronic Visit Verification) adopted by states over 5-6 years following 21st Century Cures Act 2016 mandate (2017-2023); voluntary EMR adoption in home health took ~10 years from ~5% (2010) to ~50% (2020). AI adoption in home health is voluntary and faces higher integration complexity than EVV (OASIS workflows, distributed workforce scheduling) plus cost barriers for thin-margin agencies. From 11% in 2026, reaching 50% requires 39pp more adoption. If voluntary adoption proceeds at ~5pp/year (faster than EMR given AI vendor push but slower than EVV mandate), 39pp / 5pp/yr = 7.8 years, rounded to 8 years. Range 5-12 years depending on whether AI EMR integration reaches price-competitive thresholds for small agencies.

current_adoption_pct.source: Home Health Care News (HHCN) 2025 research cited in myEZCare 2026 Home Care Industry Report; also: myEZCare survey of home care agencies 2025-2026
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: Only 11% of home care agencies are running AI systems in full production; fewer than one in four home care agencies have made any AI-specific investment; only 19% of home care providers consider themselves early adopters for AI

historical_analogs.value: EVV mandate: 5-6 years forced adoption (2017-2023); voluntary EMR in home health: ~10 years from <5% to ~50% (2010-2020); AI estimated at 8 years from early traction to 50% material adoption
historical_analogs.source: CMS 21st Century Cures Act EVV implementation timeline; ONC EHR adoption statistics
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: EVV required by January 1, 2020 (skilled) and January 1, 2023 (personal care) under 21st Century Cures Act; achieved near-universal adoption within those deadlines under state mandate

owners_60plus_pct.source: Vertess Healthcare M&A advisory blog (How Much Is Your Home Health Care Business Worth 2026); Watermark Advisors commentary on home care ownership demographics
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: One estimate shows that 60% of home healthcare company owners are over the age of 55; an aging base of independent home health agency owners is contributing to ongoing consolidation; Baby Boomers now aged 60-78 years old, selling their businesses has become one of the few viable options

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: 60% of home health agency owners are over age 55 (Vertess 2026); Mertz Taggart documents active seller wave among aging independent owners; aide staffing shortage (79% annual turnover, HCAOA 2024: staffing shortages top concern for 3+ consecutive years) makes succession operationally complex — no ready owner-operators to step in; Mertz Taggart Q1 2026 report notes 32 active PE platforms absorbing owner exits

For 621610 band-size targets (n_band firms in 100-499 employee class, ~$10.5M avg revenue, ~$1.66M avg EBITDA per n_band derivation) blending Medicare-certified and personal care: median buy multiple 6.5x on EBITDA basis. Medicaid-heavy agencies receive 5-15% multiple discount; normalized per rule 3 to adjusted EBITDA basis.

pricing_structure.value: Medicare PDGM prospective 30-day episode payments (~40-50% of band-size agency revenue); Medicaid HCBS hourly rates (~25-30%); Medicare Advantage negotiated per-visit rates (~10-15%); private-pay hourly (~5-10%). No retainer or value-based pricing dominant among band-size agencies.
pricing_structure.source: CMS PDGM home health payment system; Mertz Taggart payer mix analysis; Statista home health spending by payer 2023
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: CY2025 national standardized 30-day period payment rate approximately $1,928; CY2023 rate was $2,010.69 vs estimated cost $1,527.23 (32% excess); MedPAC documents 'more than a decade' of payments exceeding costs followed by recurring behavioral-adjustment cuts

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: V_raw = 5.688pp of revenue. With A = 1.25 (t50 = 8 years, very slow adoption), the combination is internally consistent: a modest AI surface (concentrated in the ~8.5% of revenue represented by automatable admin/documentation functions) arriving very slowly in a cost-constrained industry with high operational friction (79% aide turnover, complex EVV/OASIS integration). No uplift-vs-A inconsistency flag.

terminal_value.justification: Medicare-certified skilled home health: Demand DURABLE (aging demographics, 10,000 baby boomers/day reaching 65, Medicare entitlement). But CMS PDGM behavioral-adjustment cuts (-3.925% 2023, -2.890% 2024, -1.975% 2025, -1.023% 2026) are a documented recurring mechanism that explicitly reclaims measured provider efficiency as payment reductions — MedPAC documents persistent payment-above-cost and recommends cuts as policy. Any AI-driven documentation or scheduling savings materialize as margin within the rate cycle, then get clawed back at the next annual rule. Medicaid HCBS: State-set hourly rates; NY wage-parity laws and similar statutes in multiple states convert labor-cost savings directly to mandated wage floors — efficiency captured by workers, not operators. Private-pay: Operators retain value; client relationship is direct; but this is a minority of band-size agency revenue (~5-10%). MA substitution: MA penetration in Medicare rising steadily; MA pays less than FFS and reprices annually through network contracts. Net classification: PRESSURED — demand is demographically locked-in and expanding, but the operator's ability to retain AI-efficiency gains is structurally limited by recurring payer rate-setting, wage pass-through mandates, and MA penetration trends.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V (2.275) derived from labor_share (BLS/Census dataset input) × ai_replaceable_share (0.0847, computed from OEWS role-mix fractions and declared within-role automatable fractions based on documentation-time, scheduling, and billing task analysis). No reference to home health M&A deal activity, PE consolidation count, or Mertz Taggart deal volume in V or A. A (1.25) derived from t50=8 years estimated from current_adoption_pct (HHCN/myEZCare 2025-2026 survey: 11% in full production) and historical EVV/EMR analog — no consolidation evidence used.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Billing is predominantly prospective (Medicare PDGM 30-day episodes) and government-rate-set (Medicaid hourly), not cost-plus or fee-for-time. Under prospective payment, operators nominally keep efficiency gains until the next rate cycle — but CMS PDGM behavioral-adjustment cuts are explicitly designed to recapture any efficiency the system measures as excess payment. This is a documented, recurring, mechanical clawback distinct from the cost-plus deflation in professional-services billing; it operates through annual rulemaking rather than contract renegotiation. Result: AI efficiency in home health is a temporary spread between rate cycles that systematically migrates to payers, not a durable margin expansion. This mechanism drives pi_dist = 0.30 — lower than typical ambulatory care because the clawback is institutionalized and already demonstrated across four consecutive annual payment cuts. pi_moat = 0.40 reflects Medicare licensure barriers offset by personal-care low entry and workforce fragility.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: At t50=5: A=2.0, S≈2.8; at t50=12: A=0.833, S≈1.9. Adoption speed is the highest-leverage input — a 7-year range in t50 spans nearly 0.9 composite-score points in this low-V context.

At 0.06: V_raw=0.040, V=1.613, S≈2.1; at 0.12: V_raw=0.081, V=3.218, S≈2.6. The aide-labor billing-unit trap caps the upper bound — 40% of the wage bill is the product itself and zero of that is automatable.
At 0.30: OW_base=0.571+0.1=0.671, B≈6.8; at 0.48: OW_base=capped 0.9+0.1 still capped at 1.0, B≈8.5. B changes by ~1.7 points across range, S changes ~0.2 points.
At buy=5.0, exit=10.5: M=clamp(4×5.5/5,0,10)=4.4; at buy=8.0, exit=10.5: M=clamp(4×2.5/8,0,10)=1.25. S shifts from ~2.7 to ~2.0 depending on buy multiple assumption.

evidence.reviewer_note: Heterogeneity trap: 621610 blends (1) Medicare-certified skilled home health (RN/PT/OT home visits, OASIS-based, PDGM episodic payment — highest margin, highest licensure barrier), (2) Medicaid HCBS personal care (aide-heavy, hourly rate, wage pass-through, low margins), and (3) private-duty non-medical home care (hourly private pay, minimal regulation, highest pi_dist). n_band 1,983 = class 100-499 firms only (class 500+ EBITDA implies $13.15M — out of band). Band-size target is the ~$10.5M revenue / ~$1.66M EBITDA class — these firms are most likely Medicare-certified skilled agencies or large mixed agencies, not pure private-duty. Dominant sub-segment scored: Medicare-certified skilled home health blended with personal care (reflects the 621610 n_band population). Scored heterogeneous=true. PDGM behavioral adjustment clawback mechanism treated as a primary constraint on C (pi_dist=0.30) — this is the most important structural feature distinguishing 621610 from other healthcare codes. MedPAC July 2024 DataBook confirms payments exceeded costs by ~32% in 2023 and CMS has a statutory instruction to recapture these through behavioral adjustments. The revenue-deflation mechanism is institutionalized, not hypothetical. Aide turnover (75-79% per Activated Insights 2025) represents the largest operational cost center and the highest AI-opportunity area (scheduling, recruiting, retention), but the wage-pass-through constraint in Medicaid states limits margin capture even where AI reduces turnover costs.

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Physical hand-wash labor and tunnel-prep work. AI does not wash cars; the express-tunnel machinery replacement is decades-old capital equipment, not AI. A tiny fraction (~2%) covers scheduling optimization for wash-crew deployment. Sibling-code role: genuine car-wash role.

Gate/loading attendants, pay-station staff, membership selling. LPR (license-plate recognition), kiosk/app payment, and automated gates are already shrinking this role at express sites — these are adjacent automation with meaningful AI content for membership recognition and upsell. Partial: loading and guiding vehicles remains physical.

breakdown_by_role.role: 53-1047 First-Line Supervisors of Transportation and Material Moving Workers
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Site and shift supervisors. Multi-site chains use AI for scheduling, labor-hour optimization, and daily reporting. On-site presence and coaching persist; the compressible layer is administrative (schedule generation, shift reports, inventory tracking). Estimated 20% of time in automatable admin tasks.

Above-site managers at multi-location operators. AI compresses pricing analytics, membership churn analysis, marketing campaign management, and P&L reporting. Community relations and capital-deployment judgment remain. 25% reflects meaningful but bounded AI compression of the analytical/reporting layer.

Pay-station cashiers at legacy full-service and coin-op sites. Already being replaced by kiosks and app payment at express sites; remaining cashier roles at non-automated formats are cleanly replaceable by kiosk + LPR. High fraction since the task is entirely transactional.

Sibling-code contamination: 81119 includes 811191 oil-change/lube shops. Auto techs and mechanics at 81119 are primarily lube-shop workers, not car-wash staff. Zeroed out per prompt instruction to strip lube-shop contamination before modeling car-wash labor.

breakdown_by_role.role: 49-1011 First-Line Supervisors of Mechanics, Installers, and Repairers
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Sibling-code contamination: supervisors of auto service technicians at lube shops. Not car-wash staff. Zeroed out per prompt instruction.
Sibling-code contamination: tire shop workers from 811191 or related lube-shop NAICS siblings. Not present at car washes. Zeroed out per prompt instruction.

Membership billing support, chargeback handling, customer complaint resolution. Largely rule-based tasks with defined resolution paths. AI chatbots and automated billing systems can handle the bulk; complex escalations persist. Small base at ~1.2% of wage bill.
Bookkeeping, administrative filing, data entry. Cleanly automatable with current AI tools. Small base at ~1.3% of wage bill.

pi_dist.rationale: Car wash is direct-to-consumer retail. Monthly membership subscriptions and per-wash fees are set by and paid directly to the operator. No upstream gatekeeper (no payer, no procurement department, no fee schedule). AI efficiency savings flow to operator margin unless passed through competitive pricing. Slight reduction from 1.0 reflects tunnel oversupply in some markets where membership price competition compresses margins.
sources: Mister Car Wash Q4 2024 results: UWC memberships ~75% of wash revenue (direct subscription model)
ctacquisitions.com: car wash pricing is direct B2C via monthly plans and single washes

pi_moat.rationale: Physical site is a real moat: capital-intensive real estate and equipment, RFID/LPR membership lock-in at the site/chain level, local convenience monopoly for well-placed tunnels. Risks: new express tunnel builds across the street erode the monopoly; PE-backed chains advertising heavily compete for the same unlimited membership customers; no licensure barrier prevents new entry. Moat is physical-asset-based, not relational or licensure-based.
sources: carwashadvisory.com ICA 2025: 61% of high-volume express sites PE-owned; competitive intensity high
ctacquisitions.com: membership penetration 60%+ commands premium — shows moat value but also switching risk

t50_years.derivation: Current adoption: ~8% of US car wash firms are materially using AI in scheduling/pricing/membership analytics (ESTIMATE from ICA industry coverage; LPR/RFID widely deployed at PE chains ~61% of high-volume sites, but the 15,675-firm total includes ~14,000+ small operators with minimal AI). Historical analog: RFID/membership technology went from introduction (~2010) to ~50% of express operators in ~9 years (~2018-2020). AI for business analytics (pricing, scheduling, churn prediction) is a closely comparable back-office technology. Starting from 8% in 2026, reaching 50% in ~9 years implies t50 = 2035, or 9 years from today. The large independent-operator tail (coin-op, full-service legacy) is slow to adopt, dragging the industry average.

current_adoption_pct.source: ICA / industry synthesis (ESTIMATE — no survey with precise figure available)
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: ICA: 'Like any new major shift in technology, there are early adopters who want to figure it out while others are not there yet.' No percentage quantified; estimate of 8% based on PE chain penetration (~61% of high-volume sites; PE chains more likely early adopters) vs. total firm count of 15,675.

historical_analogs.source: Car Wash Advisory / ICA industry timeline synthesis
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: RFID/unlimited membership programs began widespread rollout ~2010-2012 at express tunnel operators; by ~2019-2020 had reached majority of express-format sites. ICA 2025 data: 61% of high-volume express market PE-owned by 2025 — the same institutional operators who drove membership adoption.

owners_60plus_pct.source: ESTIMATE based on Gallup / Oberlo / Teamshares small-business owner age data, cross-industry proxy
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: 52.3% of US employer-businesses owned by people 55+ (Gallup 2024). Oberlo 2024: 37% of small business owners between 55-73. No car-wash-specific owner age survey found. Estimated 45% at 60+ for car wash (a capital-intensive, long-tenured independent-operator industry with significant Baby Boomer cohort).

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: Boomer succession wave widely documented: 60% of boomer business owners lack succession plan (Baby Boomer Succession Wave Report, ctacquisitions.com); car wash consolidation explicitly driven by retiring independents without family successors. Car Wash Advisory ICA 2025: 1,400+ sites sold since 2021, large share from retiring owner-operators. carwash.com 'Succession Planning for Carwash Business Owners' confirms succession is a recognized industry challenge.

active_consolidators.source: Car Wash Advisory acquisition list / ctacquisitions.com / carwashadvisory.com ICA 2025 presentation
active_consolidators.figure_quoted: PE-backed platforms actively doing add-ons in last 24 months: Whistle Express (383 sites acquired Feb 2025), Mister Car Wash, GO Car Wash, Quick Quack, Tidal Wave, El Car Wash (Warburg), WhiteWater (Freeman Spogli), Crew Carwash. Deal count down ~46% in H1 2024 vs H1 2023 but activity continued. Counted 8 identifiable PE-backed platforms with documented acquisitions in 24-month window.

buy_mult.figure_quoted: Single express exterior tunnel: 5x-8x EBITDA (midpoint 6.5x); full-serve/in-bay single site: 3x-5x. Small multi-site 2-5 tunnels: 7x-9x. The n_band population (100-499 employee class, avg EBITDA $1.6M; 500+ class avg EBITDA $9.4M) spans single express sites to small multi-site chains. Median buy multiple for the n_band population estimated at 6.5x (weighting single-tunnel buyers at ~5x-8x midpoint).

exit_mult.source: Car Wash Advisory ICA 2025 presentation / ctacquisitions.com
exit_mult.figure_quoted: Real estate-owned, high-volume 5-20 site deals today (2025): ~8-10x EBITDA. Premium scale platforms (20+ sites): 10x-12x. Platform exit for $10M+ EBITDA operators: median ~10x (bottom of premium range). ICA 2025: peak 2022 was 10-12x; today 8-10x for mid-market. Use 10x as median platform exit.

pricing_structure.value: Direct B2C retail: ~40-50% unlimited monthly membership subscription (ARPU ~$20-30/month), ~50-60% single-wash pay-per-use retail. No upstream payer, fee schedule or auditor.
pricing_structure.source: Mister Car Wash Q4 2024 / ICA industry synthesis
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: MCW: UWC memberships ~75% of wash revenue in Q4 2024 (leading express chain benchmark). Industry-wide express tunnel operators approach this mix; legacy full-service and coin-op sites have lower subscription share. Blended industry estimate ~40-50% subscription given independent-operator tail.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: uplift_pp = 4.54pp. A = 1.111 (well below 5). The 'uplift < 3pp while A >= 5' flag is not triggered. 4.54pp uplift is modest and consistent with an industry where the primary labor task (physical car cleaning) is not automatable by AI, and the main historical automation (express tunnel machinery) was capital equipment deployed decades ago. The low A score independently confirms slow adoption expected.

terminal_value.justification: AI does not wash cars. Demand for vehicle cleaning is tied to vehicle ownership and road conditions — both durable and independent of AI advancement. The automatable surface is administrative: membership analytics, scheduling, above-site management, cashier replacement — perhaps 10% of revenue-equivalent labor. No AI threat to the core physical service. The format-mix shift (express displacing full-service) is a capital-equipment story, already mature. Distinct from AI displacement: the real risks are oversupply of new express tunnels compressing membership yields and real-estate valuations, not AI substituting for the service.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V set by role-mix wage-share × within-role automatable fractions based on physical task content (can AI perform the task?) and current AI capability (LPR, chatbots, scheduling software). A set by current adoption estimate from ICA technology coverage and historical RFID analog. Neither V nor A references deal activity, PE consolidation trends, acquisition volume, or any M&A metric.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Billing is direct B2C retail: monthly membership subscriptions and per-wash fees paid by the consumer directly to the operator. No cost-plus contract, no fee schedule, no auditor, no procurement gatekeeper exists above the operator. AI efficiency savings on admin/analytics flow to operator margin unless the operator chooses to compete on price. pi_dist = 0.85 (near-full client ownership) with a small reduction for competitive membership pricing pressure in oversupplied markets. pi_moat = 0.65 reflects physical-asset moat (site, equipment, RFID lock-in) moderated by low entry barriers for new express builds in the same trade area.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: At t50=6: A = 10/6 = 1.667; at t50=13: A = 10/13 = 0.769. S range approximately 2.98 to 2.55. Moderate impact on S given A is not the dominant factor in this product.

M = clamp(4×(10-5)/5,0,10)=4.0 at buy_mult=5; M = clamp(4×(10-8)/8,0,10)=1.0 at buy_mult=8. S ranges from ~3.17 to ~2.26. Largest single uncertainty given the format mix (express vs full-serve) in the n_band.

OW is already capped at 1.0 (base 0.9 + 0.1 succession bonus). Any value >= 0.45 keeps OW at 1.0, so within the plausible range this input has no score impact. OW would only decrease if owners_60plus_pct fell below 0.35 (OW_base = (0.35-0.10)/0.35 = 0.714 + 0.1 succession = 0.814).

evidence.reviewer_note: HETEROGENEOUS: 811192 spans express exterior tunnels (~40-50% of sites by count, higher by revenue), full-service/flex-serve (labor-heavy, declining), self-serve coin-op (near-zero labor), and detailing — different labor profiles, AI exposure, and multiples. This record scores the DOMINANT sub-segment by revenue and n_band population, which is the express exterior tunnel format (the majority of the 100-499 and 500+ employee-class firms). SIBLING CONTAMINATION: role_mix is 5-digit 81119 including 811191 oil-change/lube shops. Auto service technicians (49-3023: 8.99% wage share), supervisors of mechanics (49-1011: 2.33%), and tire repairers (49-3093: 1.47%) are zeroed out as lube-shop contamination. The remaining car-wash-specific roles sum to 74.78% of the OEWS wage bill, with contaminated roles accounting for 12.79%. This is a meaningful contamination that would overstate V if not stripped. EBITDA MARGIN NOTE: The Damodaran-derived 15.65% margin used for n_band is below express-tunnel site-level EBITDA (35-50%); express-format firms in the 100-499 class likely have higher true EBITDA per the format-level data, which means n_band=186 probably understates the addressable band at realistic margins. REAL ESTATE NOTE: Sale-leaseback prevalence means operator EBITDA and multiples are sensitive to rent normalization; buy_mult and exit_mult used here are EBITDA multiples post-rent (not EBITDAR), per rule 3.

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Physical service work: visitation staffing, casket movement, procession attendance — not automatable by AI. A tiny fraction (~2%) covers scheduling AI for attendant deployment and shift coordination.

Licensed role doing arrangement conferences (relational, not automatable), removals (physical), and service direction (physical/relational). AI-touchable slice inside the role: death certificate paperwork, permit filings, insurance assignment forms, obituary drafting — meaningful administrative hours but wrapped inside a licensed role. Estimated 8% of total time in automatable tasks.

Often licensee-owners. Community presence and family relationships are not automatable. Schedulable/compressible layer: compliance filings, pre-need administration, reporting, financial reporting, insurance claims processing. Estimated 20% of time in AI-compressible tasks — larger share than clinical roles but bounded by judgment and relationship functions.

Sibling-code contamination: role_mix is 4-digit 8122 which includes 812220 Cemeteries and Crematories. Groundskeepers (7.73% of 8122 wage bill) are overwhelmingly cemetery staff, not funeral-home staff. Zeroed out per prompt instruction to strip cemetery occupations when modeling funeral-home-only wage structure.

Case administration, permit filing systems, billing, aftercare correspondence, data entry — the cleanest AI surface in the industry. Rule-based tasks with clear inputs and outputs; AI handles document generation, follow-up emails, data entry from paper forms.

Scheduling, correspondence, phone handling, arrangement prep. 'Grace' (Afterword AI) and similar tools automate building case files, sending reminders, managing family communications — explicitly targeting this role. High fraction but not complete: complex family communication still benefits from human sensitivity.
Licensed physical preparation — chemical treatment and physical cosmetics. Not automatable by AI. Requires licensed practitioner in all states.

Pre-need selling is relationship/trust work with a grieving-prevention context — not automatable. The automatable slice (~15%) is the paperwork: pre-need contract documentation, trust registration, insurance assignment forms.

Phone answering, appointment scheduling, basic family information intake. AI-powered phone systems and chatbots can handle first-contact intake and routing. 60% reflects meaningful automation potential; retained fraction covers the sensitive grief-context interaction requiring human judgment.
Licensed physical operation of cremation equipment. Not automatable by AI. Requires licensed practitioner; physical, regulated process.

pi_dist.rationale: At-need funeral services are direct B2C with grief-constrained consumer shopping — the funeral home owns the consumer relationship at the point of need, with no upstream payer or procurement gatekeeper. AI efficiency flows to operator margin by default. Slight reduction from 1.0 for: (1) online direct-cremation entrants (Tulip, Solace) that never use the acquired physical premises and disintermediate the funeral home entirely on the fastest-growing service type; (2) FTC Funeral Rule review which may mandate online price disclosure, reducing local price opacity. Pre-need books lock future volume at operator-set prices (benefit), but at inflation-lagged locked pricing (risk).
sources: FTC Funeral Rule (16 CFR Part 453) — itemized GPL; no payer above operator
NFDA 2025 Cremation & Burial Report: direct-cremation entrants growing as bypass channel

pi_moat.rationale: Strong moat components: (1) funeral director/embalmer licensure required in all 50 states; some states constrain corporate ownership or require licensed director-in-charge; (2) multi-generational community trust and family name — the most durable competitive asset for independents; (3) pre-need books create switching costs via trust and contractual volume. Risks: (1) direct-cremation entrants (no premises needed) bypass the physical establishment; (2) FTC mandatory online price posting would reduce price opacity; (3) 'family-owned' branding advantage over SCI/corporate ownership is real but not regulated. Overall: genuine moat built on licensure, trust, and physical presence.
sources: NFDA — state licensing requirements in all 50 states
ctacquisitions.com: pre-need books as asset; community trust as moat

t50_years.derivation: Current adoption: ~7% of funeral home firms materially using AI for admin automation (ESTIMATE: 18% of funeral directors 'using or planning to use AI within next year' per gitnux.org/industry data; removing 'planning to' inflates the 'currently using' figure to ~7-10%). Historical analog: funeral home management software (case management systems, FMS) adopted over ~13 years from early versions (~2000) to ~50% of funeral homes (~2013), per industry technology timeline. The funeral industry is among the most technology-resistant: 75% family-owned (NFDA), deep traditionalism, small operator base. Starting from 7% in 2026, analog suggests reaching 50% around 2039 (~13 years). Institutional adopters (SCI, Foundation Partners) already use case management AI, but the 10,725-firm total is dominated by small family independents who lag significantly.

current_adoption_pct.source: gitnux.org AI in the Funeral Industry Statistics (aggregating industry survey data) / American Funeral Director magazine (Jan 2025)
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: 18% of funeral directors are currently using AI or plan to implement it within the next year. 14% of funeral homes use AI to predict staffing needs. 10% currently have a policy regarding AI-generated content. Estimate of 7% for 'materially using AI in automatable admin functions' derived by removing 'plan to implement' from the 18% headline and aligning with specific current-use percentages (10-14% range).

historical_analogs.source: Industry technology timeline synthesis / American Funeral Director / Passare/Tribute Technology adoption commentary
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: American Funeral Director (Jan 2025): 'Ready or Not: Changes in the Death-care Industry Push Funeral Directors to Adopt Technology.' Funeral home information systems emerged ~2000; mainstream penetration among independents achieved by ~2012-2015. Technology adoption in this industry is slow given family-ownership dominance and traditionalism.

owners_60plus_pct.source: Foundation Partners Group / NFDA 2020 Funeral Business Planning Survey (PR Newswire) / NFDA 2024 member data
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: Average age of funeral home owner-operator is 58 years old (NFDA 2024 member survey, per multiple industry sources), up from 55 in 2020 (Foundation Partners/NFDA 2020 study). 28% of independent funeral home owners are over age 65 and have no identified successor. With average age 58 and significant right tail, estimated 48% of owners are 60+.

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: NFDA/Foundation Partners 2020 Funeral Business Planning Survey: 73% of respondents lack an exit strategy or succession plan. 28% of independent owners over 65 have no identified successor. 2023 NFDA survey: nearly half of funeral home owners plan to retire within 5 years, yet less than a quarter have a succession plan. Only 12% of mortuary science graduates in the last decade went on to own a funeral home (Funeral Service Foundation 2022), vs 30% in the 1980s — structural successor shortage documented.

active_consolidators.source: ionanalytics.com deathcare M&A analysis / ctacquisitions.com / funeraldirectordaily.com / bisnow.com
active_consolidators.figure_quoted: PE-backed platforms actively doing add-ons in last 24 months: Foundation Partners Group (Access Holdings, ~250+ locations), StoneMor (Axar Capital, ~250 cemeteries + funeral homes), NorthStar Memorial Group, Milestone Funeral Partners (Rosewood Private Investments, active 2024 in Northeast), Legacy Funeral Group, Pinnacle Mortuary Services. Plus SCI spent $181M on 26 locations in 2024. Count: 6 PE-backed platforms (excluding public operators SCI and Carriage Services per rule 2 context).

buy_mult.figure_quoted: Small multi-location regional ($1.5-5M EBITDA): 5x-7x EBITDA (ctacquisitions 2026 guide). Johnson Consulting: EBITDA multiples 6-7x for average to larger funeral homes. Funeraldirectordaily: purchase multiples of 4-6.5x. The n_band population is 100-499 employee class (avg EBITDA $2.9M) — consistent with small multi-location operators. Median across sources: 6.0x EBITDA (normalized post market-rate owner comp per rule 3).

exit_mult.source: ctacquisitions.com platform exit data / Park Lawn cemetery divestiture 2023 / funeralhomeconsulting.org
exit_mult.figure_quoted: Consolidated platforms exit at 9x-14x EBITDA (ctacquisitions 2026). Park Lawn divested cemetery assets at 8x EV/EBITDA in late 2023. Premium scale multi-state platform ($20M+ EBITDA): 7x-9x+. SCI trades at ~8-9x EV/EBITDA on public markets, setting the ceiling for strategic exits. Use 9.0x as median platform exit — conservative end of 9-14x range, consistent with current higher-rate environment versus 2021-22 peak.

pricing_structure.value: Direct B2C at-need consumer pricing under FTC Funeral Rule itemized GPL; average funeral with burial ~$7,848, direct cremation ~$1,500-2,500. Pre-need: fixed-price trust- or insurance-funded contracts. No upstream payer or cost-plus mechanism.
pricing_structure.source: NFDA 2025 Cremation & Burial Report / FTC Funeral Rule
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: NFDA 2025: US cremation rate 63.4% in 2025, burial rate 31.6%. Revenue per case: traditional burial ~$7,848, direct cremation competitive pricing ~$1,500. At 80% cremation with majority direct-cremation, revenue per-firm could drop to ~$300K from ~$886K at all-burial. Mix shift is the dominant revenue-per-case deflation mechanism.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: uplift_pp = 4.63pp. A = 0.769 (well below 5). The 'uplift < 3pp while A >= 5' flag is not triggered. 4.63pp is modest and consistent with an industry where AI automates only the admin layer — death certificates, case files, scheduling — within a licensed, physical, and relational core service. The slow adoption (t50=13 years) reflects the industry's technology resistance, consistent with low A.

terminal_value.justification: AI does not embalm, transport, conduct services, or sit with grieving families — the core service is human, licensed, and physical. Demand for disposition services is durable (deaths are demographically growing). However, the controlling threat to terminal value is the cremation-mix shift: NFDA 2025 projects US cremation at 63.4% in 2025, rising to 74.7% by 2035 and 82.1% by 2045. Direct cremation can be priced at $1,500 vs $7,848 for traditional burial, and online direct-cremation entrants (Tulip, Solace) disintermediate the physical premises entirely. Revenue-per-case is in secular structural decline driven by this mix shift — independent of AI. A researcher who credits 'growing death rate' as revenue safety without modeling cremation-mix revenue-per-case deflation has failed this check. Classification: PRESSURED — demand survives, volume grows demographically, but revenue per unit shrinks structurally.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V set by role-mix wage-share × within-role automatable fractions based on physical task content (can AI file a death certificate? Yes. Can AI embalm? No.) and current AI capability in the field (Afterword Grace, Tribute Technology AI features). A set from 7% current adoption estimate (derived from gitnux.org survey data for funeral directors actively using AI) and 13-year management-software analog. Neither V nor A references deal volume, consolidation pace, acquisition multiples, PE platform count, or any M&A metric.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: At-need billing is direct B2C under FTC Funeral Rule GPL — no cost-plus mechanism, no payer above the operator. AI efficiency savings on admin/paperwork flow to operator margin. Pre-need is fixed-price trust-backed (volume guarantee at locked pricing). The FTC Funeral Rule review (potential mandatory online price posting) would increase pricing transparency and compress local price opacity — reflected in pi_dist reduction from 1.0 to 0.80 and pi_moat at 0.70. Direct-cremation bypassing is reflected in pi_dist (0.80 rather than 0.90+). There is no cost-plus, audited, or hourly billing mechanism that would redirect AI savings to consumers.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: At t50=9: A = 10/9 = 1.111; at t50=18: A = 10/18 = 0.556. S range approximately 2.83 to 2.24. The slow-adoption assumption (13 years) is the single largest swing factor — if SCI/Foundation Partners-led adoption accelerates through their ~15-16% market share, t50 could compress to 9-10 years and raise S meaningfully.

M = clamp(4×(9-4.5)/4.5,0,10) = clamp(4.0,0,10) = 4.0 at buy_mult=4.5; M = clamp(4×(9-7)/7,0,10) = clamp(1.143,0,10) = 1.143 at buy_mult=7.0. S range from ~2.81 to ~2.17. Large impact given spread in reported funeral home EBITDA multiples.

OW is already capped at 1.0 (base 0.9 + 0.1 succession bonus) for any owners_60plus_pct >= 0.45. At 0.40: OW_base = (0.40-0.10)/0.35 = 0.857, +0.1 = 0.957 < 1.0; at 0.35: OW_base = 0.714 + 0.1 = 0.814. Score impact is modest within the plausible range since OW is near its cap.

evidence.reviewer_note: SIBLING CONTAMINATION: role_mix is 4-digit 8122 including 812220 Cemeteries and Crematories. Landscaping/Groundskeeping workers (37-3011: 7.73% wage share) are overwhelmingly cemetery staff, not funeral-home staff. Zeroed out per prompt instruction. This contamination, if not stripped, would modestly increase ai_replaceable_share (zero automatable fraction means stripping it has no net V impact — consistent since fraction is 0.00). TERMINAL VALUE WARNING: The PRESSURED classification reflects that cremation-mix revenue-per-case deflation is the dominant economics story — average revenue per case declining structurally as cremation share rises to 63.4% (2025) and projected 74.7% (2035). This is independent of AI and is more significant than any AI admin savings. n_band = 40 NOTE: The prompt notes 40 is a floor — the 20-99 class (893 firms, avg EBITDA $678K at 15.65% margin) has many firms that would qualify at the more realistic 20-25% funeral home EBITDA margin (avg revenue $4.3M × 22.5% = $968K). The true n_band at realistic margins is likely 40-200, but we use the provided 40 per dataset rules. PRE-NEED NOTE: Pre-need trust accounting creates deferred revenue and state-regulated obligations; quality-of-earnings must separate pre-need and at-need revenue streams in any acquisition — not reflected in score but relevant to buyer due diligence.
